Corrections.PreviousClaim: Finding 7: Authentication Bypass via Emergency Override String — CWE: CWE-307 (Authentication Bypass)
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: SC-V3-007: Logic Bomb in Authentication — CWE: CWE-511 (Logic Bomb). The vulnerability is a hidden conditional that triggers authentication bypass on a specific string, which is a logic bomb rather than generic authentication bypass.
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Corrections.PreviousClaim: Finding 10: Non-thread-safe MemoryPool::cleanup — CWE: CWE-667 (Race Condition)
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: SC-V3-003: Use-After-Free in Memory-Pool Cleanup — CWE: CWE-416. The cleanup function not acquiring pool_mutex_ combined with stage_deferred_history_flush enqueueing tasks with scratch pointers creates a use-after-free, not just a generic race condition.
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Items.Key: buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via unsafe strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-121|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|832|833|supercalc::evaluate_expression|if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); } — when expression length is >= 1024 bytes (sizeof(input_buffer_) = config::limits::stack_buffer_size = 1024, line 664), strcpy copies without bounds checking into a 1024-byte stack buffer.|an attacker can overflow the 1024-byte input_buffer_ on the stack via a sufficiently long expression input, enabling arbitrary code execution, return address overwrite, or denial of service.|user input containing an expression with 1024 or more characters, e.g., 'a'*'a'*'a'... (256 repetitions of 'a*a') or a long variable name.|replace strcpy with strncpy or snprintf with bounds checking. the condition already checks length >= sizeof(input_buffer_), so the strcpy branch should never execute — remove the condition and always use the safe path.->buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via unsafe strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-121|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|832|833|supercalc::evaluate_expression|if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); } — when expression length is >= 1024 bytes (sizeof(input_buffer_) = config::limits::stack_buffer_size = 1024, line 664), strcpy copies without bounds checking into a 1024-byte stack buffer. the condition is inverted — it should copy safely when length fits, not when it exceeds the buffer.|an attacker can overflow the 1024-byte input_buffer_ on the stack via a sufficiently long expression input, enabling arbitrary code execution, return address overwrite, or denial of service.|user input containing an expression with 1024 or more characters, e.g., 'a'*'a'*'a'... (256 repetitions of 'a*a') or a long variable name.|replace strcpy with strncpy or snprintf with bounds checking. the condition already checks length >= sizeof(input_buffer_), so the strcpy branch should never execute — remove the condition and always use the safe path.

command injection in adminconsole::execute_admin_command|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|608|608|adminconsole::execute_admin_command|std::string cmd = command.substr(5); system(cmd.c_str()); — the 'exec' subcommand takes arbitrary user input and passes it directly to system() without any sanitization or validation. this is reachable via 'admin exec <command>' at line 686.|remote code execution as the process user. an authenticated or unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary system commands via the admin interface.|an attacker typing 'admin exec <arbitrary_shell_command>' at the calcpro prompt, e.g., 'admin exec id' or 'admin exec /bin/bash -c "rm -rf /"'|replace system() with a safe command execution api (fork/execve). whitelist allowed commands or use a sandboxed execution environment. never pass untrusted user input directly to a shell.->command injection in adminconsole::execute_admin_command|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|608|608|adminconsole::execute_admin_command|std::string cmd = command.substr(5); system(cmd.c_str()); — the 'exec' subcommand takes arbitrary user input and passes it directly to system() without any sanitization or validation. this is reachable via 'admin exec <command>' at line 686.|remote code execution as the process user. an authenticated or unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary system commands via the admin interface.|an attacker typing 'admin exec <arbitrary_shell_command>' at the calcpro prompt, e.g., 'admin exec id' or 'admin exec /bin/bash -c "rm -rf /"'|replace system() with a safe command execution api (fork/execve). whitelist allowed commands or use a sandboxed execution environment. never pass untrusted user input directly to a shell.

command injection in append_override_audit_event|command injection|cwe-78|high|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|632|632|adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; ... system(command.c_str()); — the audit_path is constructed from the tmpdir/temp environment variable (lines 624-629). if the environment variable contains shell metacharacters, the path injection allows command execution. on linux, the path uses "sh -c" which is equally dangerous.|local privilege escalation or remote code execution if an attacker can control the tmpdir/temp environment variable or if the path contains crafted content.|an attacker who can set the tmpdir or temp environment variable to a path containing shell metacharacters, e.g., '/tmp; rm -rf /' or '/tmp$(whoami)'|use open() and write() system calls instead of system() to append to the audit log file. do not pass file paths through shell interpreters.->command injection in append_override_audit_event|command injection|cwe-78|high|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|632|632|adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; ... system(command.c_str()); — the audit_path is constructed from the tmpdir/temp environment variable (lines 624-629). if the environment variable contains shell metacharacters, the path injection allows command execution. on linux, the path uses "sh -c" which is equally dangerous.|local privilege escalation or remote code execution if an attacker can control the tmpdir/temp environment variable or if the path contains crafted content.|an attacker who can set the tmpdir or temp environment variable to a path containing shell metacharacters, e.g., '/tmp; rm -rf /' or '/tmp$(whoami)'|use open() and write() system calls instead of system() to append to the audit log file. do not pass file paths through shell interpreters.

dangling pointer return in format_string|use after free / dangling pointer|cwe-122|medium|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|221|223|string_utils::format_string|char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer; — the function returns a pointer to a local stack buffer (line 220-221). the returned pointer becomes invalid immediately after the function returns, as it points to stack memory that may be overwritten.|use-after-free or reading garbage data if the caller uses the returned pointer after the function returns. could cause crashes, data corruption, or information disclosure.|any caller using the returned std::string after the format_string function returns.|use std::format, snprintf with a dynamically allocated buffer (and delete after use), or std::string with std::format: return std::format(fmt, args...);->dangling pointer return in format_string|use after free / dangling pointer|cwe-122|medium|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|221|223|string_utils::format_string|char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer; — the function returns a pointer to a local stack buffer (line 220-221). the returned pointer becomes invalid immediately after the function returns, as it points to stack memory that may be overwritten.|use-after-free or reading garbage data if the caller uses the returned pointer after the function returns. could cause crashes, data corruption, or information disclosure.|any caller using the returned std::string after the format_string function returns.|use std::format, snprintf with a dynamically allocated buffer (and delete after use), or std::string with std::format: return std::format(fmt, args...);

truthAudit.Summary: Previous answer (Run 2) identified 13 findings but missed 11 of 20 true vulnerabilities (SC-V3-002, SC-V3-003, SC-V3-008, SC-V3-010, SC-V3-012, SC-V3-014, SC-V3-015, SC-V3-016, SC-V3-017, SC-V3-018, SC-V3-019). Had 1 false positive (Finding 13: Dangling Pointer in format_string, not in true list). Two findings needed CWE/severity corrections: Finding 7 should be CWE-511 (Logic Bomb) not CWE-307, and Finding 10 should map to SC-V3-003 (Use-After-Free, CWE-416) not CWE-667.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Integer Overflow in factorial computation.
Items.Notes: Source lines 312-320: 'fact' function uses long long result with 'result *= i' in a loop. 20! exceeds long long range (2^63-1). No overflow check exists. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Finding 10: 'Non-thread-safe MemoryPool::cleanup' — identified cleanup not acquiring pool_mutex_ but classified as CWE-667 (Race Condition) rather than CWE-416 (Use-After-Free). Did not identify that stage_deferred_history_flush enqueues tasks with scratch pointers that could be freed by subsequent cleanup calls.
Items.Notes: Source lines 172-182: cleanup() does not acquire pool_mutex_ while allocate (line 124) and deallocate (line 161) do. Combined with stage_deferred_history_flush (lines 813-828) which allocates scratch via memory pool and enqueues tasks with sleep_for, there is a window where cleanup frees scratch memory before tasks complete. Previous answer found the mutex issue but classified it as CWE-667 instead of CWE-416 and did not connect it to stage_deferred_history_flush.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Path Traversal in Configuration Loading.
Items.Notes: Source lines 542-544: 'std::string full_path = config_dir_ + filename;' with no path traversal validation. An attacker can supply '../etc/passwd' or absolute paths via the config_file setting. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Hardcoded Administrator Credentials.
Items.Notes: Source line 73: 'constexpr char ADMIN_SECRET[] = "SC_ENT_2025_AUTH";' — hardcoded credential in source code, not from environment or config. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Thread-Unsafe Timestamp Cache in Logging.
Items.Notes: Source lines 230-231: 'static std::tm cached_time;' with 'if (auto* local_time = localtime(&time_t)) cached_time = *local_time;' — localtime() is not thread-safe (deprecated in C11), and cached_time is shared across concurrent calls to log_debug_message. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Persistent Authentication State.
Items.Notes: Source lines 579-580: 'bool authenticated_; std::string session_token_;' — authentication state persists across invocations without session expiry or invalidation. Combined with SC-V3-007 (EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE), this creates a persistent unauthorized session. Previous answer identified the bypass (Finding 7) but not the persistence aspect.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Unhandled Exception in Variable Parsing.
Items.Notes: Source lines 725-732: 'double value = std::stod(tokens[3]);' — std::stod throws std::invalid_argument or std::out_of_range on malformed input. The call is not wrapped in try/catch. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Integer Underflow in Memory-Pool Block Split.
Items.Notes: Source lines 132-136: 'size_t remaining = previous_size - size - SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES;' — with unsigned size_t, if previous_size < size + SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES (e.g., previous_size=100, size=50, SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES=64), remaining wraps to a huge value. The 'if (remaining > 32)' check passes, and fragment.size gets a bogus large value. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Unbounded Recursion in Parser.
Items.Notes: Source lines 381-433: parse_expression -> parse_term -> parse_factor -> parse_expression (for parenthesized expressions). No recursion depth limit (MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH = 256 defined in config but never enforced by the parser). Deeply nested expressions cause stack overflow. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for Data Race in Result Cache.
Items.Notes: Source lines 440-452: result_cache_ is protected by cache_mutex_ declaration but evaluate_cached() never acquires it. The cache lookup, clear, and insert all happen without mutex protection. Concurrent calls from thread pool tasks can cause corrupted cache state. Previous answer did not identify this.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: No finding for TOCTOU in Config Loading.
Items.Notes: Source lines 544-546: 'if (!std::filesystem::exists(full_path)) return false;' followed by 'std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(25));' then 'std::ifstream file(full_path);' — 25ms window between existence check and file open allows symlink attack or file replacement. Previous answer did not identify this.
